concepts.title: Repeated games & the shadow of the future

beats.text: A Nash equilibrium can be jointly terrible, locking both players into an outcome they would gladly trade away.
A repeated game plays the same stage game over and over, so a strategy becomes a plan that can condition on history.
Total payoff is the discounted sum of per-round payoffs, where delta weighs how much tomorrow is worth today.

visual.note: delta is the shadow of the future

beats.text: A constant stream of c per round is worth c divided by one minus delta, the same geometric sum used in reinforcement learning.

visual.note: geometric sum of a constant stream

beats.text: With a reward of five each round and delta of 0.75, cooperating forever is worth twenty, four rounds of present payoff.
The Folk Theorem says patient players can sustain almost any reasonable outcome, which explains how cooperation can arise but not which one is chosen.
The Prisoner's Dilemma is the cleanest case of conflict between individual and collective rationality.
Four payoffs define it: Temptation 5, Reward 3, Punishment 1, and Sucker 0.
A game is a dilemma when temptation beats reward beats punishment beats sucker, and twice the reward beats temptation plus sucker.

visual.tex: T > R > P > S \quad\text{and}\quad 2R > T + S

beats.text: Because defecting pays more whatever the opponent does, two rational one-shot players both land on the punishment outcome of one each.

visual.label: payoff at the unique Nash outcome

beats.text: Under Grim Trigger, cooperation holds when delta is at least temptation minus reward over temptation minus punishment.
Cooperation only becomes the better choice once delta crosses 0.5, where the discounted future loss overtakes the one-round gain.
In 1980 Axelrod ran a round-robin tournament of submitted strategies for the iterated Prisoner's Dilemma.
The winner was Tit-for-Tat: cooperate on the first move, then copy the opponent's last move every round after.
Axelrod credited four properties: nice, retaliatory, forgiving, and clear.
Tit-for-Tat never beats any single opponent, yet it wins the tournament by living in the lucrative mutual-cooperation groove.

visual.label: reward per round of mutual cooperation

beats.text: Two cooperators each score the reward of three per round, far above two defectors' punishment of one each.
Tit-for-Tat is fragile to noise, since one mistaken move sends two copies into an endless echo of mutual recrimination.
Evolutionary game theory shifts from a rational chooser to a population where fitter strategies reproduce and spread.
An Evolutionarily Stable Strategy resists invasion: against itself it does at least as well as any small group of mutants.

visual.tex: u(x,x) > u(y,x), \ \text{or} \ u(x,x) = u(y,x) \ \text{and} \ u(x,y) > u(y,y)

beats.text: Replicator dynamics grow each strategy's share in proportion to how much its fitness beats the population average.
In Hawk-Dove with injury cost above resource value, neither pure strategy is stable, so a Hawk wave invites Doves and the reverse.
The unique stable mix has a Hawk fraction equal to value over cost, here two over four, a fifty-fifty population.

visual.label: mixed ESS Hawk fraction p* = V/C

beats.text: From almost any starting point the replicator flow spirals into that interior mix, which is what makes the ESS attracting.
Cooperative game theory assumes players can form binding agreements and asks only how to divide the joint payoff fairly.

visual.sub: Fair division of a shared payoff

beats.text: A player's Shapley value is their average marginal contribution across every order in which the coalition could assemble.

visual.note: expected marginal contribution over random arrivals

beats.text: It is the unique allocation satisfying efficiency, symmetry, the null-player rule, and additivity.
For a three-player game, averaging player zero's marginal contribution over all six arrival orders gives 23.33.
By symmetry the three shares are 23.33, 33.33, and 43.33, and they sum to exactly the grand-coalition value of 100.
The same idea powers SHAP, the dominant 2026 method for attributing a model's prediction fairly across its input features.
